steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank sits between the subject ("Florida-based researchers Martha A. Scholl, Maoya Bassiouni, and Angel J. Torres-Sánchez") and the rest of the predicate ("compiled climate data from several sites"). The verb must connect directly to what follows without any interruption.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — compiled,: A comma after a transitive verb cuts it off from its direct object ("climate data"). No punctuation belongs between a verb and the object it governs. ✗
  • Choice B — compiled: No punctuation. The verb flows directly into "climate data from several sites," its direct object. Standard English requires this bond to be unbroken. ✓
  • Choice C — compiled;: A semicolon must be followed by an independent clause. "Climate data from several sites…" is not a clause at all — it is a noun phrase completing the verb. ✗
  • Choice D — compiled:: A colon introduces a list or elaboration that is grammatically separate. Here the material after the blank is the object of the verb, not a separate elaboration. ✗
That matches Choice B — compiled.
